comments.title: Rituals for protection of areas
comments.content:
Hello,

I am wondering about rituals for protection of places and whole areas. Is there such a ritual that has been especially effective in your experience? Preferably a ritual which can be tailored to whatever place and tradition is appropriate. I am in a nature based tradition that probably would be close to or at least in the same philosophical framework as druidry. Were do I find a good description of something like this? I would guess that you probably have written a book or two with such a ritual in it?

replies.title: Re: Rituals for protection of areas
replies.content: The larger the area that a working is intended to affect, the more magical skill it takes to make it work, and so protective rituals for anything larger than (say) a room, or your own personal space, normally can't be done effectively by anyone who isn't already skilled enough to design such a working on their own. It's also very difficult to make something with the flexibility you require.  Here again, the usual practice is to craft a unique ritual specific to the place and tradition in question. Sorry.

replies.title: Re: Rituals for protection of areas
replies.content:
Dear Fredrik,

When I read your question, the first thing that came to my mind was the thing JMG mentioned, "How big is this place?" I agree that the larger the area, the more skill and understanding is required to do a protective ritual which will be helpful.

A few bits of advice.

I think it would be presumptuous to do a ritual to protect a place on land or sea if you have never been there yourself. It might make more sense to give material support a group that is already involved in trying to protect it, if that group contains individuals who have visited the place or live there. Your support could be donation of money, supplies, or volunteer work.

You could identify a being who would be interested in protecting the land in question, make an offering to the being, point out this place to the being and ask them to give the place protection.

If you have visited the place and it means something to you, the next question that arises is how to tell the difference between offering the kind of protection it wants and being an interfering busybody. Same as when you are acquainted with a person who seems to be danger. The land is alive and has consciousness, the natural features within it are alive and have consciousness, it contains innumerable inhabitants, some would say the rocks are conscious and there are dragon currents, etc.

Beyond a general blessing, getting advice would be in order.

For example:

1. You might do a divination on the question "What does this land need?" and/or ask someone whose divinatory abilities you respect to do a divination. Divination First is a good maxim before doing magic that will affect others.

2. Are there are indigenous people with traditional ways who live around there and who might be doing rituals to protect it? Inquire whether they would like supportive prayer from a non-indigenous person. If so, they will tell you what to pray for. Or they may ask you for a donation, in which case, you probably should give them one. If you don't get a good feeling from the interaction, don't pursue it further.

3. If you have time and some empathy for wordless communication with non-humans, you might take a solitary walk on the land, sit under a big healthy tree that has beneficent vibes, or by the bank of a year-round stream, or put your back against a boulder that seems friendly (in a spot that doesn't have a lot of biting insects). Try to tune in to the consciousness of the spirit of that tree/rock/waterway. If it is open to your contact, you might ask it to tell or show you something about the history of the place and what the place needs.
If you get a reply, bear in mind that you are not consulting an oracle. You are getting the POV of one long time non-human inhabitant. They may have something to say which is worth listening to, but it isn't the whole story.

comments.title: Pentagrams in the CGD + Kybomancy
comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer,

Well, you'll need to tell us what that piece of old occult literature was, as it may provide an efficient cure for sleepless nights :-D

I have two (totally unrelated) questions this week.

1. If I'm not mistaken, in the Celtic Golden Dawn the Pentagrams of the 4 Elements are traced on their own, i.e. without being preceded by the Equilibrating Pentagrams of Spirit, unlike in the classical Golden Dawn on which the CGD is modelled. Is there any particular reason for this difference?

2. Looking for a suitable oracle as part of my FHR training, I recently stumbled across the book Fortune-Telling by Dice: Uncovering the Future through the Ancient System of Casting Lots, by D. & J. Line. Though I haven't tested it yet thoroughly, I must say this book stands out. It is well written, and its methods seem promising. Above all, its dice symbolism is really one of a kind: as far as dice divination (kybomancy) goes, I haven't seen anything like it before.

However, though originally published in the 80's, I can't seem to find any information on this system anywhere on the Internet, e.g. the source of its peculiar dice symbolism. Do you (or the Commentariat) happen to know more about this system and/or its authors?

Thank you as always for hosting this space.

replies.title: Re: Pentagrams in the CGD + Kybomancy
replies.content:
1) That's quite correct. If you read the discussion of pentagram rituals in The Celtic Golden Dawn you'll notice that the pentagrams themselves are different. One consequence is that there's only one Spirit pentagram, not two, since in the CGD system the pentagram that invokes air doesn't banish water! Another is that the equilibrating pentagrams, which are needed in the classic GD system to hold the somewhat ramshackle architecture of the pentagrams together, are not necessary once the pentagrams are changed.

2) Fascinating -- I've never heard of that before. The term "kybomancy" evoked all the wrong associations in my mind, as when I was a kid in the Boy Scouts "kybo" was Scouting slang for "outhouse," and I confess I was dreading finding out how you use one of those for divination!

replies.title: Re: Pentagrams in the CGD + Kybomancy
replies.content:
On 2), unfortunately, I don't have anything on Line or his(?) work, but if you find dice congenial, I thought I might point you in a potential direction for exploration that you might not have considered: tabletop roleplaying games and the discussion around their design. Specifically, games have played around with a lot of different ways to pay attention to dice and interpret their results, in order to get different probability spreads or different "feels." Understanding the probabilities involved can also help give a sense for what results are likely, what rare, and so forth, which might be useful in understanding the significance of any particular result.

A few things about dice that you may or may not know or find helpful:

a) Dice come in all sorts of weird and wonderful shapes beyond the traditional cube. Since many of these are the same shapes as the Platonic solids, that could be a source of occult symbolism to meditate on.

b) If you have a second or subsequent roll that depends on the roll before it, you multiply the probabilities together. For example, the probability of rolling a 6 and then another 6 on one re-rolled die, or two rolled sequentially is 1/6 * 1/6 = ~.028.

c) If you roll multiple dice and add the results together, the outcomes will follow a normal distribution over enough rolls, which makes certain numbers more common than others. For example, on the familiar 2d6 (two regular six-sided dice rolled and added together, like in Craps), the number 7 is the most common, 6 and 8 are slightly less common, 5 and 9 slightly less so, and so on, until 2 and 12 are the least likely results to be rolled. Knowing this can greatly improve your "Settlers of Catan" placement strategy, but is also helpful if you want a system where more common results come up more often, but less common results aren't unheard of. The more dice you add together, the more likely you'll get something close to the mean and the less likely you'll get something out on the tails.

d) Not on dice particularly, but I'd check out sources on numerology and number symbolism more generally to see if that's where Line is getting some of his stuff. My understanding is that number symbolism became especially popular in the Muslim world after initial influence from the Hellenistic world, and then flourishing because it was abstract and therefore didn't go against prohibitions on depictions of/interactions with personalized forces that aren't Allah or His angels. Islamic Patterns by Keith Critchlow goes into this for its implications on physical artwork (think mosaics), which I haven't read in years, but from what I remember almost certainly shows a lot of Pythagorean/Hermetic influence.

Anyhow, hopefully some others have stuff more direclty related to what you're looking for, but maybe some of this will be interesting or helpful despite being pretty far afield.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: Carl Jung the Occultist
comments.content:
As part of my current occult studies, I've been slowly making my way through some of Carl Jung's works. I'm trying to prepare myself to read his Liber Novus. One thing I've quickly realized is how little the actual Jung resembles the version that many "Internet Jungians" seem to talk about (where they usually like to reduce his thought to some kind of reductionist materialism with a spiritual flavoring). Can you recommend any books or articles that actually discuss his work as the occultist he seems to have actually been?

Any suggestions from the commentariat are also welcome.

replies.title: Re: Carl Jung the Occultist
replies.content: I originally got clued into the occult side of Jung by W.E. Butler's Magic and the Qabalah, which cheerfully discusses a few of the parallels between Jung's system and the ceremonial magic Butler practiced. Stefan Hoeller's The Gnostic Jung and the Seven Sermons to the Dead has more to say about it. Another pair of fascinating books are Richard Noll's The Jung Cult and The Aryan Christ. These two should be read with more than one grain of salt, as they were intended to denounce Jung -- "OMG, he's an occultist! An occultist, I tell you!" is basically what Noll was shouting. Some of us responded by rolling our eyes and saying, "About time you noticed," but orthodox Jungians have cattle when you mention those books. As you say, most of them are reductionist materialists who want a safe venue in which to play at being spiritual.

replies.title: Re: Carl Jung the Occultist
replies.content: Quite right about Richard Noll's two books!  Shallow and very disappointing ...

replies.title: Re: Carl Jung the Occultist
replies.content: I found them oddly entertaining, in a droll way. The quality of their thought and analysis was, as you say, utterly shallow, but despite himself Noll kept on mentioning bits of information that made me more interested in what Jung had to say.

replies.title: Re: Carl Jung the Occultist
replies.content: Thank for the suggestions, JMG!  Hoeller's work was already on my reading list, but I'll also add the others.  As a side note, it amuses me when skeptic types like Noll unwittingly help to advance the very thing they're rejecting!

replies.title: Re: Carl Jung the Occultist
replies.content:
In a possible synchronicity (heh, given the topic), I just read an excerpt of and ordered the somewhat unfortunately-titled Transforming Darkness: A Shadow Work Toolkit for the Red Pilled Initiate, but it hasn't arrived yet, so I can't speak to its overall content. It seems to take the spiritual implications of Jung's work seriously from what I've seen, but obviously it's fairly focused on Shadow work specifically, so maybe of limited usefulness.

Also, if you have the time and inclination to share any "lessons learned" on approaching Jung from an occult/spiritual perspective, even as basic as "where should I start?" I would certainly appreciate it, and I suspect some others might as well. I've only approached him piecemeal so far, but he's on my (too long) list for similar reasons, and any guideposts along the way from someone with similar goals would be most welcome.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Carl Jung the Occultist
replies.content:
I can tell you what I've been doing. For my basic reading list, I've adopted the books suggested by JMG for getting prepared to read Liber Novus: https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/171302.html?thread=27081254#cmt27081254

I've also acquired two copies of the Red Book: the facsimile edition that includes full reproductions of the artwork that Jung created, and a reader's edition that lacks those. I've only very briefly skimmed the book itself, and have mostly focused on deeply concentrating on some of the artwork. I have these two different copies because I think it actually makes it easier to work with the Liber Novus.

I'm otherwise still so early in this study that I can't offer much in the way of even intermediate insights or suggestions. I will say that I've come to the conclusion that Jung is the closest to a 20th century "prophet" that I've found. He was able to dive deep into the occult aspects of reality and human nature, and the "messages" he writes down in his books, most especially Liber Novus, deserve to be recognized as among the greatest spiritual gems to come out of the 20th century.

replies.title: Re: Carl Jung the Occultist
replies.content:
Thanks very much for this!

I forgot to mention, though he may well fall into your category of internet Jungians pitching reductionist materialism with a spiritual flavoring, that I have found Jordan Peterson's Maps of Meaning (and his various lectures) a useful, well, map to the archetypes. Like most maps, its value is in its simplification, so much of the richness of Jung's actual thinking on archetypes is lost, and he very much tries to ground everything in materialism, so that might be a turn-off.

If you'd like to get more of a feel for it, and if I might be a bit immodest, I wrote up a summary on it here: https://jpowellrussell.com/#jordan_peterson_s_framework_for_myth

Cheers, and continued luck in your endeavors,
Jeff

comments.title: Eternity and such
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

What is the origin of an individual soul/Monad...what prompts its genesis into the mineral or sub-earthly/elemental kingdom? Does this go on for eternity, or will there be a time when new Monads are no longer created? My spinoff question from this is, although you seem to affirm individual progress/evolution/ascension through kingdoms and life-waves, do you hold out any faith/belief in collective progress/redemption of all creation over the long sweep of eternity? Even if brand new souls are perpetually created, I don't quite see how there is no collective/net progress, because at the "other end" of the spectrum of evolution souls are progressing indefinitely/infinitely. For "net zero" balancing to be true would we not need to assume that souls at the "upper end" of evolution reach an impasse beyond which they either stop progressing or cease to exist?

On another topic, what are your thoughts on the alleged apparitions of the Blessed Mother approved by the Catholic Church in places such as Fatima and Medjugorje? Several esoteric authors state that they are authentic and that she is now a spiritual master, yet at the same time her (very traditional Catholic) message in some ways contradicts their own views of reincarnation and karma, universal salvation/redemption. I know people deep in esoterica and the occult who are simultaneously very devoted to these apparitions. Is this trying to have it both ways? No one (including these very said people) has been able to answer this to my satisfaction...it's often glossed over or avoided.

Thank you kindly for your thoughts and ideas.

Anthony from Pittsburgh PA

replies.title: Re: Eternity and such
replies.content:
1) The universe is so vast in space and time that we have no way of judging any of this. The teachings I follow hold that here, in this solar system, in this cycle of time, souls emerge from the divine plane as waves of unformed soul-stuff, descend down the planes to the material plane, and begin the long journey upwards from there. Most of what we know about, and most of what occult traditions teach, has to do with the process by which we, as souls currently in human incarnation, can learn the lessons and master the challenges of this one stage of being, and move on. How does all this work out in eternity? A dust mite in one of Mozart's wigs had a better chance of understanding his music than we do of answering that question. As Eliphas Lévi pointed out, that's the kind of thing where knowledge has to give way to faith -- not in the sense of accepting an ideology, but in the sense of simple trust.

2) Religious apparitions are quite common in times of social stress, and they consistently reflect the beliefs of the people who witness them. That doesn't mean that there's no real spiritual presence there -- it means that everything reported from such apparitions has been filtered through human minds. Since Marian apparitions are far from the only kind, their testimony needs to be weighed against the testimony of other apparitional beings, from Hindu deities to flying saucer occupants. As I suggested in my book A World Full of Gods, when all sides have the same kind of evidence, and all those lines of evidence point in different directions, arguing that only one such line of evidence should be taken seriously is dishonest logic.

replies.title: Re: Eternity and such
replies.content:
You didn't ask me, but I am sure at least one apparition was fabricated to convert the locals, especially after the success of Guadalupe in Mexico. For example, if you are up for a small tale since I have no other argument than an intuition. I was in Costa Rica a few years ago celebrating my parent's 3 decade marriage. Incredibly beautiful country, amazing people, nature spirits the speak in-between branches and way too many predatory tourists making the locals miserable. Nowhere else I've seen such Potemkin charade been put up to so that tourists can ignore poverty in their happy vacay. Which takes me to the next scene. I was strolling down a "commoner" street as I do when I get fed up of turisty things, shuffling in my mind fantastic ideas of a Thaumaturgical Order of St. Cyprian when I entered the steps to a museum. Amazing stuff, truly amazing, their native tribes had a very refined sense of the place they inhabited and they showed it in their golden artworks. There was even a hall, I wonder if it was intentional, dedicated to the four elements plus spirit. Well, that's how I read it, there were four symmetrical displays holding animals made out of gold that very clearly stood as the four elements --including a salamander with dancing flames!-- and a center piece talking about the gods and goddesses.

I came out of the room a little elated, after doing a thing with eyes closes in front of a piece that stood up to me and suddenly, I noticed a black, dark metaphorical cloud above a one story, "colonial" building (mesoamerica was never a place of colonies, for as much as the dramacrowd of appropriation and decolonization like to insist and project into it, it was a place of viceroyalties). So I went into the building, it was an exhibit on slavery, and the story of an attempted apparition (I suspect). Apparently, the Spanish crowd was unsatisfied with social situations, so they were looking at places to establish a church, but not just any church, one that would replicate the effects created in Mexico by Guadalupe. They found a place, built a church, fabricated an apparition and that year the church came down from an earthquake. It was rebuilt, and the next year another earthquake took it down... Is the Blessed Virgin trying to protect her name? I don't know, but it certainly looked like it! In my experience the contradictions made in text by the Church et all (which comes more from the protestant side of things, in terms of the virgin btw) is because it got political, any practitioner can tell you that she is indeed powerful and active.

As for your question, being a catholic and a polytheist occultist, my only comment is try and see. She is there and this time of year especially as it is the time when she housed the disciples after the crucifixion.

replies.title: Re: Review of Paths to Immortality and Strengthening the Thumos
replies.content:
Speaking as a relative neophyte (CGD Bard, frequent reader of these Magic Mondays for a few years) but as someone who needed similar help with thumos recently and got inspiration here, here are a few experiments I found worked well. Two are very simple, taken from prior Magic Monday suggestions, and one is more complex, adapted from the CGD Ovate tree working:

(1) Lighting blue candles during Jupiter's planetary hour on four consecutive Thursday days, lighting red candles during Mars's hour on five consecutive Tuesday nights

(2) Lifting weights with other men on Tuesdays, Thursdays, and Saturdays (planetary hour timing did not seem to matter much for me here compared to day timing)

(3) Taking an iron implement as a sign of a Martian-type striving and bold deity (in my case sometimes a knife and sometimes a bracelet) to sit against the trunk of an oak as a sign of a Jupiterian-type benevolent ruling and supportively paternal deity, observing the insects and animals for a minute as they take risks and handle risks, as they threaten and attack one another and handle the threats and attacks, letting these exchanges (such as a spider lunging for an ant or being caught by a crow) be the signs of a Saturnian-type uncompromisingly lawful and exacting deity, and then with those three signs and deities thus present, meditating (still sitting with back to the tree) on the evolution, ecology, and philosophy of fear and courage with the hope of developing well-tempered, steady thumos "that will hold up even in the worst Jupiter-Saturn-Mars squares and oppositions"

In (3), the "squares and oppositions" are meant symbolically and should develop in meaning and application through the meditation. For instance, a worst case / crisis case of "when my own debts cut against my ability to protect others" might correspond to a Saturn-Jupiter opposition with Mars square to both. Also, re (3), I don't know if it works without doing the lead-up CGD Ovate work.

I hope those are of some help!

replies.title: Re: Re:
replies.content:
Have you tried not overthinking? :-)

I'm just teasing you a bit. I say this as an inspiration not as a description but in my experience to avoid becoming one of the proverbial Gray People from Michael Ende --which is not exactly the same as the "people without chests", but since thumos also refers to the emotions and passions in a way-- one has to do what Momo did and find a way to bring back the stollen time to the people. How to tonify thumos without it becoming the rage the drove Achilles to murder soldiers with nothing in his eyes and to drag Hector's body behind his chariot; and instead become wisdom, virtue and truth? To me, thumos rises by aiming at higher ideals, and to me those are expressed in art. Having something to protect and defend which becomes dear, almost falling in love with, "philosophical love", not somatic love, by aspiring to what I understand is philotimia (love of honour). There is an expansion that needs to be directed. In Romans 15:20 Apostle Paul made the preaching of the good news of the Gospel his philotimo.

One thing I've noticed in my project leader for example is that when you are in a position of responsibility you kinda want to keep track of everything, to the point that most if his time is wasted in things that can be safely ignored as they will either come back if important or nobody cares, but his OCD kicks in and literally doesn't let him. Why? Because he is relating rationally to the project (very important) in order to deliver, instead of focusing on the creative "why" we are doing it. Which is technically my job but he could use some of it for sure. You don't plan for that, you create an environment around you that lets you be at ease and connect, to allow the juices to flow. "Shiva created the manifested world so that Shakti could dance within it's boundaries and experience his totality" Why are we doing all this planning if it is a boring project, I told my lead once. Nobody will want to use that. I can't deliver what you want if you don't make time for me to go learn and experiment with something cool and apply what I get to learn in our project. It kinda worked, he even got involved with my toy project and enjoyed hands-on programming again. The demo was fun, we got funding and now we have a team of 10 which he can merrily manage while I direct the design aspects of it.

...it's like this. Sometimes, when you've a very long street ahead of you, you think how terribly long it is and feel sure you'll never get it swept. And then you start to hurry. You work faster and faster and every time you look up there seems to be just as much left to sweep as before, and you try even harder, and you panic, and in the end you're out of breath and have to stop--and still the street stretches away in front of you. That's not the way to do it.

You must never think of the whole street at once, understand? You must only concentrate on the next step, the next breath, the next stroke of the broom, and the next, and the next. Nothing else.

That way you enjoy your work, which is important, because then you make a good job of it. And that's how it ought to be.

And all at once, before you know it, you find you've swept the whole street clean, bit by bit. what's more, you aren't out of breath. That's important, too...
[...]
People never seemed to notice that, by saving time, they were losing something else. No one cared to admit that life was becoming ever poorer, bleaker and more monotonous. The ones who felt this most keenly were the children, because no one had time for them any more. But time is life itself, and life resides in the human heart. And the more people saved, the less they had.”


It reminds me of figure drawing. If you approach it rationally, you get a beautifully rendered, anatomically perfect, atelier-style drawing that says nothing. Loosen up a bit, don't see the line and don't feel your hand, look at the person in front of you, touch the paper with the tip and let it do its thing. Your eyes know how to see, your hands know how to move, just "delegate" and let them do their things while the brain is having a feast of sensations looking at the gorgeous naked model in front of you. Look back, correct, and repeat.

Just my random stream of thought, not saying this is exactly your case.

Do you like contemporary jazz? I'm very fond of Go Go Penguin. Let it seep you in and you'll have sensations bouncing of your skull man! I think having that easiness of sensation to be good with striving for greatness, and it takes reps. Have you felt how a song first listen is ok, give it 5 and by the 6th you are singing with it? It's like using music to flex the sensations and once you know the pattern you can invoke it. After such a session I feel like I can conquer the world and take an importunity to jot ideas. It feels like changing gears a little bit, stress drops to the floor, juices are allowed in, and the intellect brightens up as a result, like opening a window on a stale room.

comments.title: Names of gods
comments.content:
Hi JMG, what do you see as the implications for worship and devotion in gods with widely publicized names compared to gods whose names are obscured in some way? The primary member in the latter category being the Abrahamic god, but perhaps there are others associated with mystery cults.

I recall reading that ancient Hebrew priests would invoke the name of their god only at certain temple rituals, then the ritual changed such that the name was drowned out by chanting and not heard by worshipers, and finally forgotten with no one left who remembered the pronunciation. This deity is now referred to indirectly with terms that mean "the name," "a god," "the lord" or in the case of Germanic languages the word "God" which is a kenning of Odin.

Commenters in the past have mentioned accounts of people receiving visions from deities like Baldr and Apollo saying that they were guiding their devotees' descendants in the guise of Christ. Could it be that a nameless or indirectly referenced godhead can serve as a proxy for prayers and worship to potentially contact multiple different divinities?

replies.title: Re: Names of gods
replies.content:
Well, first of all, a very large number of divine "names" are titles and euphemisms rather than personal names. We don't know the actual name of any of the old British or Gaulish gods, just the titles people used to avoid speaking the names -- Cernunnos means "he of the horns," Epona means "she of the horse," Rhiannon means "great queen," and so on. Similarly, the "names" Zeus and Jupiter both come from an old word for bright light, with the latter having "father" added to it -- "the shining father" isn't a bad translation -- while Hera simply means "the lady."

Second, we do have the name of Jesus Christ, and yet I've noticed that people who pray to that name seem to be addressing a whole gallimaufry of different beings. The god of unconditional love that some Christians worship doesn't have much in common with the pale, judgmental god of death at war with biological life that other Christians invoke. So whether or not the name is public doesn't seem to matter much.

replies.title: Re: Names of gods
replies.content:
Speaking of title, some think that “Jesus” is actually “J-Zeus”or “I-Zeus”, and not a translation of “Joshua”. The letter J is a modern invention and didn’t exist in the time the Greeks wrote the New Testament books.

“Christos” is Greek as well, meaning anointed.

Taken literally it could mean “ I’m the one anointed by Zeus".

replies.title: Re: Names of gods
replies.content: In Greek it's Ἰησοῦς, and Zeus is Ζεύς, so very few of the letters are the same, nor is that combination of vowels pronounced the same. Nor is "I" the first person pronoun in Greek or Aramaic!

replies.content: Though I had a Catholic friend, long ago, who liked to insist that the Mexican name Jesús was actually "Hey, Zeus!"

comments.title: Demons as metaphors
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

We use symbols to communicate with our sub-conscious minds. To what extent in your assessment are demons symbols and metaphors, and to what extent are they real? And is there a difference?

Kindly,
Boy

replies.title: Re: Demons as metaphors
replies.content: As Goethe pointed out, "Alles Vergängliche Ist nur ein Gleichnis" -- "all that is transitory is but a symbol." We can only experience symbols, never realities. Thus demons are symbols and metaphors, but so are you, and so am I, and so is Joe Biden. What actual reality lies behind the image is all but impossible to judge, but the universe does seem to work as though malevolent spirits have some kind of actual, concrete existence.

replies.title: Re: Demons as metaphors
replies.content:
If I may add an opinion:

An intention to communicate with "your own" sub-conscious mind sometimes bleeds over into affecting things as though it was an intention to communicate with (certain of) the sub-conscious-mind-like qualities of the process that is generating the apparent universe. I don't know what principles govern when this bleedover happens or doesn't happen. But I think those principles are closely related to the principles on which practices such as magic or prayer work in the first place.

In Christianity at least, it is commonly supposed that demons are often the sort of being that would look for excuses for clinging to someone by coming up with convenient stretched interpretations of the intentions behind the person's choices. That premise seems like a reasonable guess to me on the basis of the evidence, and more so for the kinds of evidence that Christians particularly tend to get. I imagine that one of the ways of inventing a stretched interpretation would be to exploit some ambiguity associated with that bleed-over: the risk is that a demon might invent some way of framing itself as a response to an intention to communicate with something that turned out to also ambiguously include the sub-conscious-mind-like qualities of the process that is generating the apparent universe. So I think there is good reason to be cautious here. But I also want to be cautious about simply endorsing the stated premise. The idea of "this is an intention you would make if you wanted a demon to interpret it in such a way as to justify clinging to you" seems to me to create a sort of psychological tar-pit of substantially artificial drama around how one should interpret one's own ambiguous intentions, and the artificial drama seems to be part of the psychological capturing strategy of Christianity in its material aspect as a memeplex superorganism. But at the same time, probably some of the drama would be well-founded at least for most people most of the time, and I don't know a safe way of moving to the sort of higher perspective of insight about one's own motivations from which such drama would reliably correctly dissolve.

From what I have read here, the risk is more acute (and the drama is, comparatively speaking, better-grounded) when the "symbol one is using to communicate with one's sub-conscious mind" is of a demon commonly understood to be real. So don't do that. (Reading between the lines as to the intention of your question -- ) Invent a fictional cosmology if you have to, and in that cosmology, avoid names or conjunctions of attributes associated with demons that people believe in. And even that still won't protect you, if or insofar as you were to have an unconflicted intention to actually invoke a power of malignant dysregulation, and not just engage in self-discovery or self-negotiation or whatever purpose you may be thinking of.

(And yes, as far as I understand, there are practices that are like such "self-discovery or self-negotiation" but at a more external level. They require significant preparation of character, contact with other benefic aspects of the universe-process (since after all the existence of other powers is how there could even be such a negotiation), and discipline; one example is training to become an exorcist. If you can't tell whether you're at that level yet, then you should assume you aren't. But it may interest you to learn that, in therapist M. Scott Peck's book "People of the Lie", he reports a patient in whose exorcism he had been involved telling him that they could see how therapy was like exorcism but in slow motion. I can see how that could make sense. I guess the idea is that an important step of most exorcisms is exposing and breaking the invader's access to falsehoods they were leaning on, about what their activity was and why that activity would be justified. That's an important thing that therapy does as well, with less power but with a lot less subtle body disruption.)

replies.title: Re: Demons as metaphors
replies.content:
... and now that I re-read that, I see that in the last paragraph I wasn't really thinking carefully about the distinction between what to say about entities (e.g. archetypes, planetary intelligences, or oppositional divine figures) that have independent power as a part of the ordinary life of this universe and just have various degrees of malefic or not-already-regulated traits, versus what to say about entities that doubled down on resisting correction of their dysregulation and are largely excluded from the life of the universe and of themselves powerless. One lesson here is that I don't entirely know what I'm talking about, since if I did, I would have remembered that good advice to give about one category of entity wouldn't necessarily be right to give about the other kind.

Also, it's arguably hypocritical for me to give advice like this here. I seem to myself be in the deep end (with respect to the first kind of entity, not the second kind) for reasons I don't entirely understand, and have been partly trying to feel out whether there was an important reason for this instead of only just trying to disengage with all haste (as would normally be required by the standard of "if you can't tell whether you're at that level yet, then you should assume you aren't"). I guess I was imagining that if I disengaged, there might not be enough other people to engage in my place, even as relatively ill-fit as I seem to be: supposedly, archetypes and so on still go on doing stuff, even when there's nobody to consciously notice and point out how the stuff they're doing is at cross-purposes with other stuff.

comments.title: Help...I am confused
comments.content:
I have been trying to figure out how to cast a mundane horoscope by hand, with printed charts and no computer aid.

I am more confused now than when I started.

Is there a reference book that is easy to read and doesn't do a lot of hand waving that can walk me through this process? I have a copy of this years ephemeris, I have the blank charts, but for some reason. the books that I have when it comes to actually casting the chart I think that I have got it all wrong.

It might just be me, but this seems more complicated than calculus. I just don't get it.

replies.title: Re: Help...I am confused
replies.content:
It's got its complexities. First of all you have to calculate the house cusps, and that's usually the difficult part: you need the longitude, latitude, time and date for which the chart is to be cast, and a book of tables that includes tables of houses. You work out the local sidereal time -- that tells you exactly what degree is rising in the east -- and from there, using the tables of houses, calculate the house cusps. You write them onto your blank chart. Then you look up the places of all the planets except the Moon and put them into the chart. Then you calculate exactly where the Moon was -- it moves 12° to 15° a day, so this takes some calculations based on the exact time -- and put that in.

Old-fashioned books on astrology typically walk you through the whole process step by step, but they assume you have what counted as an eighth-grade knowledge of math in 1920, which is more than most college graduates have these days. See if you can find a book such as Chart Your Own Horoscope by Ursula Lewis, that goes through the process step by step. Alternatively, the American Federation of Astrologers (astrologers.com) has a correspondence course that covers all the math, with tutors to help you when you get bollixed up.

replies.content:
I'll work on the Lewis book first. I am just trying to figure out the algorithm. Math ain't the problem, I was a B+ student through four quarters of calculus, so it isn't the math.

comments.content:
Hello JMG! Thanks for another Magic Monday.

My question is about self initiations and what they really do. Is it possible to actually get self initiated after following a solo study program from a book in a way that we achieve the "7th death" as Fortune describes in Cosmic Doctrine? Are self initiations ever real initiations or just "self dedications?" Thanks again!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
All initiations are ultimately self-initiations -- going through a ritual conducted by other people helps give you a boost, but you still have to do most of the work yourself. You can certainly do a self-initiation -- it's just going to take you more work.

Let's go a little deeper. Initiation awakens one or more levels of your subtle bodies using some specific set of symbols and the transmission of a spiritual energy. In a ritual initiation, that happens all at once, because the initiator or initiators use ritual drama to focus your attention on the symbols, and transmit to you the energy they have already received themselves. You can do exactly the same thing via self-initiation -- you just have to study and meditate on the symbols, and invoke the spiritual energy. This takes more time, and often multiple repetitions of the process, but it has the same effect.

I'll use an example from my own experience. I originally self-initiated into the Golden Dawn tradition. When I finally received temple initiation, there wasn't anything new about it for me -- the energies and the egregor of the order were more or less saying, "Oh, hi, welcome back" as I went through the ritual!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Got it! Thanks a lot! :)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi again, I'm the guy who asked the original question. If I may ask one more thing: what path of study did you take to self-initiate in the golden dawn tradition? Will I achieve similar results (not in the standard GD tradition, of course) following through the Golden Section books or your Celtic GD book?

Thanks again!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) My approach to the GD system was limited by the books available to me at the time -- basically, Israel Regardie's books The Middle Pillar, Ceremonial Magic, and The Golden Dawn. I made the LBRP and Middle Pillar exercise my daily ritual practices, did the ritual work in Ceremonial Magic regularly, and then worked my way through everything in the big doorstop book, meditating on all the texts and symbols and teachings, and performing all the rituals and exercises, cover to cover.

2) The complete Golden Section sequence will have similar effects, once it's done -- there will be seven books in the main series, and probably four additional volumes as well. The Celtic Golden Dawn is obviously less extensive, but if you combine it with The Mysteries of Merlin, Coelbren, and The Litany of the Tree of Life, that will have a comparable effect.

replies.content: There are a number of European traditions, like the Martinist Order, that are very firm in their insistence that their initiation HAS to pass from a person who has authority to transmit it to the candidate. In this I think they have a lot in common with empowerments in the Vajrayana Buddha sense as well as initiations in the Sufi orders. However both of these traditions have allowances for spontaneous transmissions from enlightened beings to those who are especially , oh, I don't know, prepared? Developed? Anyway, I don't know of any corresponding wiggle room in the European Orders I'm thinking of. I am in two of those lineages (three, if you count the gnostic church), and I've been giving a LOT of thought to this issue, especially recently.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may, I've also been considering this issue, as I received Martinist initiation last year, and of course I've also been consecrated as a Gnostic bishop. The Martinists certainly have the right to define their own initiatory lineages however they wish, as do the various churches, but I suspect there are plenty of people who attain the same spiritual states without benefit of formal initiation.

As for the Vajrayana, I was interested to learn a little while back about the empowerment of the bodhisattva Cundi (Zhunti, Juntei), which is conferred by hearing her mantra once. Here it is, if you're interested! Of course once you receive it, if you want to go further, you have to put in the time doing the necessary practice. But it's interesting that even in so structured a tradition there's at least one wholly respectable transmission that is passed on so casually, without formal ritual.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I was going to say something elseabout this as a stand-alone comment, but decided "Hey, it's not my blog", but, in a nutshell (really, much less than I was typing previously):
Yes, I agree that those who hold the lineage get to decide how the lineage can be passed on, and their decision holds, as long as they actually have the inner plane mana to actually BE a lineage holder. So Martinism has to be transmitted by a person duly authorized to transmit it.
That said, I think if EVERY ONE of the Martinist initiators (or whatever school we're considering, I think this holds across the board) were to all be gone from the physical plane, a person who truly wanted to resurrect the lineage would have help from the graduates of the school on the other side and it would be possible. After all, the spiritual transmission had to make the jump from the spiritual plane to the physical without the intervention of an incarnate person originally to begin with.
There is a story that, after the passing of Martinez de Pasqualez, a number of his Elu Cohen went to his successor Willermoz and demanded that he transmit to them the Reaux Croix, the highest degree of the Order. Willermoz told them that he hadn't seen any member of the Order who had manifested the requisite spiritual standing (there were actual specific signs that were looked for, called "Passes", which initiates had to report before they would be advanced.), but, not to worry, for, if God could raise up children to Abraham from the stones, he could certainly create Reaux Croix if it was called for.

Partially this is thanks to attending my first Pagan Gathering in quite some years last month, partially because you mentioned the idea of get-togethers for this community, and partially just because I'm a pretty solitary person, and it seems like a good thing to do as I get older. So, it got me thinking. How would you go about hosting multiple people in a GSF Lodge? To the best of my knowledge, you haven't published anything on the topic yet, and GSF has a very solitary-first slant to it, so I assume it will come eventually. But I was curious. Would it work much like the AODA Grove, where participants would be in place before the consecration begins, or would there be a processional into the space after it's established?

Second, as I work through things, I had a bit of a revelation on discursive meditation this week. I think I'm tending to break up my meditations into sections, breaking out of the depth of it when I fall into it to get distracted, then feel like I'm starting over from scratch to get back into the meditation. How can I work on getting better at staying in it. Or should I consider longer sessions in the hopes I get into a longer one later? Currently I do 10 minutes of meditation, or around 16 minutes total with relaxation and fourfold breath. Thoughts I have include trying a different posture for a while, either lying down or sitting with my back on something, or walking meditation where rather than sitting still, I walk in a non-distracting natural environment as I go. I also intend to spend some time in a more eastern style meditation as well, because that is harder for me, but if I persist, I tend to have more breakthrough mindfulness periods than I do with discursive. And as I type, I realize I should also try scrying more often, as it engages the brain in a different way than DM and might be helpful. I haven't done a scrying session in several months. Anyway, any other suggestions?

As usual, thanks for hosting these. And as implied above, don't drop the idea of get-togethers. And if anyone's in the Washington DC area and wants to get together for a coffee or pint, or try to organize a smaller regional event, message me here on dreamwidth.

The Dilettante Polymath

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The first task is to get the bee out. If it lands on any surface, get a glass and a small piece of thin cardboard. Put the glass over the bee, trapping it, and then slide the cardboard under it. Explain out loud to the bee that you know it's lost and you're just putting it back outside. Then carry it outside and let it go. comments.title: The Arch Effect
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A local bit of weather lore is called the Arch Effect. The arch being referred to is the Gateway Arch in St. Louis, MO. It's over 600 feet tall, made of stainless steel, in the shape of the curve that a flexible line takes under gravity when one holds each end. It and its grounds constitute one of the US' most visited national monuments.

The Arch Effect takes its name from an apparent tendency for storms to form a kind of "eye" around the Arch, so it won't be raining at the site of the Arch but it will be raining all or most of the way around it. If there is a protective effect, it does eventually break down, but it happens often enough for us residents to notice.

Recently I learned that the designer of the Arch was employed by the three-letter agency on weather modification at around the same time as he was designing the Arch. That puts the local lore into an interesting light.

Sometime this summer I'll visit the grounds to take in the subtle energy. This is a crowded place so I can't do anything at all obvious. Could you offer some advice on how to read subtle energies in a crowded place? We can walk right up to the Arch and put our hands on it or lean against it; many people do, so I would not draw any undue attention by doing so. The legs point north and south.

Thanks for your tips!

Not JMG, but in case it helps:

First off, my condolences. Secondly, a lesson I am still learning that came up in response to losing my mom a few years ago is that grief/sadness/"bad" feelings of all sorts are not necessarily (only) problems to be solved. Sure, if you are so bummed out you can't function in day-to-day life, you likely need to handle that, but sadness is unfortunately a normal part of life, and just "getting it" intellectually doesn't take the place of feeling it and letting it take it's course. The insight that really helped me get a different perspective was this: of course you're sad: something good has been lost. It would be weird not to feel bad, a denial of the good that was there when you had it.

I know that's not much immediate comfort. For that, if you have a relationship with any kind of kind, forgiving, comforting deity/saint/other spiritual being, you might consider praying to Him or Her - for me, that has helped with some of the most acute episodes of sadness. If you don't already have such a relationship, well, the good news is that such deities tend to be pretty welcoming by definition!

Good luck, and my blessings if you welcome them,
Jeff

comments.title: Dealing with awful stress
comments.content:
So I’m in this horrendous housing situation that has dragged on for many months and which I alone must deal with while others are more or less helplessly on depending on me. As I’ve complained elsewhere, the lenocratic property management “industry” are acting as though they yearn to destroy us.

I’m not asking for a solution to all that. It’s in the hands of the gods. What I do need is a way of coping with the horrendous levels of stress which all this engenders. Above all I need a way, or ways, to maintain a positive mindset while dealing with some very difficult existentially menacing situations.

I have some background in Golden Dawn, Hellenic variety, including my own answer to the Rose Cross ritual. So there’s that. Is there anything else you could recommend to restore and maintain peace of mind even while objective circumstances do not seem to warrant it?

replies.title: Re: Dealing with awful stress
replies.content: Get regular full-body massages. Those will help decrease the muscular tension, which can turn into a source of feedback loops. Also, if you aren't already doing so, may I suggest a daily devotion to Athena? She helped Odysseus through a lot of stressful events, and can do the same for you.

replies.title: Re: Dealing with awful stress
replies.content:
Years ago, I received a helpful piece of advice years ago about stress. When we are stressed or worried we often can imagine really bad things happening - it's not uncommon for a parent seeing their kid playing to easily see a danger and imagine how it could result in death. So for other stresses, it's easy to imagine losing a home or a job. Often though, on the positive outcome side, the most we imagine is things staying the same or a minor win. It can help with stress to purposefully imagine positives as drastic as the negatives. I.e. If worrying about being evicted, imagine on the other hand the company could fold and the owner offers to sell the house for a bargain, or a distant family member could offer you a home. Likely the reality lies somewhere between the two and balancing them helps with overwhelm from the fears.

It can also be helpful to daydream or write the story of a postive outcome to your worst fear, for the above example, one could think of a goal they have that being evicted would seemingly prevent or impede; then daydream or write a couple stories in which being evicted is part of achieving that goal. Help yourself to see that the worst is survivable, and the best is (narrowly) possible, and the stress becomes more manageable.

comments.title: Blessings
comments.content:
Hello,

I have been doing a version of the Blessing Walk where I say "I offer you my blessings" at random times. Mostly while driving when I see people and then I started doing it when I found I was mad at people, to help shift my focus so I didn't spiral into a negativity loop. It's always in the form of an offer.

I have noticed I get feelings back from people. The most common ones are:
-A hug
-A high five, most common, I think this is people who are generally nice but not religious
-less common, a really advanced form of love or return blessing I can't define

And then the negative ones:
-a nasty little dance number to shove my offer back in my face
-a nasty dance followed by weeping
-just weeping, like they are helpless on the floor with despair

I almost never get a blank or neutral back. In one case, I was upset about the state of the world and I offered a blessing to my country's leader. Many say he has dementia if that narrows it down. In that case, I got the most violent return feeling I have ever gotten, like trying to strangle me. I have never had anything like that, even when offering blessings to people who are clearly having mental trouble in my town. It was definitely an offer, I don't think I crossed any permission boundaries. I tested this by offering my blessing to other political figures and got some of the most common reactions but not this violent one. It's a real anomaly.

I'm wondering if this is weird or in the ballpark of normal and if other people experience it?

comments.title: Immortality
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
I am currently rereading your Five Rites book. It struck me how many strands of the background occultism you discovered and explained emphasized the goal of immortality. I understand that most people fear death, but it seems to me that an actual spiritual practice should prepare one for the next step rather than being the spiritual equivalent of freezing halfway up a tall ladder. Christians and other believers in eternity with their God have many examples of persons who prayerfully welcomed the transition to the afterlife. On the other hand, belief in reincarnation seems to take two forms--the Hindu or Buddhist practices focus on release from the cycle of rebirth, while Westerners seem to look forward to another ride. I realize this is a gross simplification of those beliefs.

But it seems to me that a desire for one's current lifetime to be extraordinarily prolonged, even if perfect health could be assured, is strange. Admittedly there are many experiences to look forward to, but also disappointment and loss. Even if you lived in a community in which everyone was doing the practices and would share you immortality there would be accidental deaths or fallings out and people in the outside world that one would mourn--a favorite author or musician who will no longer produce, for example. And without unlimited income many of the desired experiences are out of reach--travel, concert tickets, books, amazing restaurant meals, and other pleasures all cost. Leaving out the prospects of natural or social disasters wouldn't there eventually be fatigue at keeping up the exercises and all the other things necessary: worrying about one's diet, being tired of the daily chores. But more importantly, I think, wouldn't one eventually tire of being oneself--the same character with the same flaws and virtues, the same talents and lacks? It just strikes me that a person would have to be really self-satisfied to want to go on forever--yet such self-satisfaction doesn't strike me as an advanced spiritual state.

I welcome comments from other readers as well as from JMG.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Immortality
replies.content: Oh, I know. I'm not in favor of physical immortality; when I get to the end of this life I expect to shrug off my body, including its defective nervous system, and my current rather awkward personality with a sigh of relief they'll hear on Jupiter. For some reason, quite a few occultists in the 19th and early 20th century didn't feel the same way -- and not just occultists: fundamentalist Christians back in the day made it one of their major talking points that Jesus was coming soon and so you don't have to die!. Both groups were wrong, of course.

replies.title: Re: Immortality
replies.content:
I wonder whether the religion of progress in 19th and 20th century Western culture contributed to a desire for physical immortality rather than purely spiritual. After all, if you believe that society started with a Golden age and has gone downhill ever since, why would you want to stick around for the worst?

But if the religion of progress is true and everything is going to get better, with humanity's destiny in the stars, wanting to live to see that would make more sense. One doesn't hear as much about it lately, but the cryogenics movement was pretty strong in some circles in the 1970s and 1980s; there were numbers of people seriously hoping that a method would be perfected to freeze people with incurable diseases until science discovered a cure. A number of people involved in the cryogenics movement were also believers in life-extension technologies. "There are people living now who will never die." This was more of the promise of science which would inevitably find cures for now fatal diseases and chronic conditions as well as discovering methods to repair old age. When you consider that technology went from Kitty Hawk to the moon in one person's lifespan with comparable changes in many fields the belief in progress made some sense. On the other hand, one might think it slightly contradictory to look for life extension secrets to come from ancient Tibetan monasteries. But people are weird like that.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Immortality
replies.content: That strikes me as a very probable hypothesis. If you think Utopia is about to arrive, why not try to be there when it shows up?

replies.title: Re: Immortality
replies.content: Further data point in support of your hypothesis: I encountered the idea of cryogenics reading about the Silicon Valley tech scene and its adjacent cultures in the 90s and 00s, so in some sense, it followed where the progress was happening and believed in.

replies.title: Re: Immortality
replies.content:
Some of these concerns are probably the reason why the legend of the philosopher's stone promised not only indefinite life, but also riches (by transmuting gold).

More to the point, there's the Greek myth of Tithonus, a man who eloped with a goddess and sought immortality. The gods, in their caprice, granted him eternal life, but not eternal youth. So, after a lovely honeymoon, he kept aging and getting older, and older, yet he could not die, eventually turning into a mummy sealed away in a tomb somewhere. Like other Greek myths, it's rather brutal, but it contains an important lesson: death has its place in the natural order of things, and as difficult as it is to accept, the alternatives can yet be worse.

A modern version of the Tithonus story is the Simpsons. Back in the 90s, when I was a little kid, it really was an amazing TV show. Seasons 2-6 were the golden age, seasons 1 & 7 were silver (when it was getting its bearings, and starting to show cracks, respectively), seasons 8-11 were bronze, aka the decline & fall, when there was good mixed with bad. Seasons 12+ are zombie simpsons, which pretends to be the same show, yet the heart, soul, and brains of it are gone; it's just a shambling husk that's gotten ever more putrid over the years. And I would draw the line there, between seasons 11 & 12, for the simple reason that the final episode of s11, Behind the Laughter, works perfectly as a final episode/sendoff (for more info, look up Dead Homer Society).

I could imagine a Simpsons fan back then wishing for the show to continue on for more decades, and they got their wish, but in a monkey's paw sort of way. The Simpsons is still on alright, but it honestly shouldn't be. For all the howling that would've ensued, it would've ultimately been better for the show to end around 9/11, that fateful day when the entire 90s zeitgeist came to a violent end. A zombie is an unnatural, evil thing -- it's alive, sort of, but at that point it's better to just give it a silver bullet and wooden stake. That's true of Tithonus, and of the Simpsons.

You probably weren't expecting a link between ancient mythology and the Simpsons, but there you have it.

comments.title: Going through the Ringer
comments.content:
Good Evening JMG,

Thank you as always for this space and your counsel.

First, I've been pondering the multiple pantheons with the SOP and was hung up trying to see how they fit. In what way are beings as different as Ced and the-planet-Saturn alike that they would both fit spirit below. It's clicked recently that spirit below itself is truly the only necessary similarity. They can both be called on when invoking solely because, in their respective pantheons, they best represent not only the powers of the spirit below, but how those relate with and balance the other powers. This shifts my conception of the SOP in general. I'm thinking of the elements now as almost like ruling astrological signs - as if Saturn and Ced are both Spirit-Belows, whereas Mars and Sul are Fires. Does this seem like a fruitful path to explore?

Second, Saturn's been on my mind as he appears to be acting forcefully within my life. One of the signposts was that I keep accidentally saying "I invoke the Spirit Below" in other elemental positions, and divination seems to point to Saturn the planet saying "Pay Attention." I'm experiencing an identity crisis of sorts. I'm seeing how much I've gone with the flow, and particularly allowed my own desires and goals to be defined by those around me, particularly family. In one way that is somewhat natural as a parent. In another, I can see that I've generally used my duties and the seemingly endless needs within a large, homesteading, multi-generational household as an excuse to not keep commitments to myself: breaking commitments to spiritual work and letting go of or shifting goals in personal artistic pursuits. I can see that, even though I thought I was being selfless, that I've become sadder, more detached, and more brittle and critical with those I love (while not setting the actual needed boundaries with them.) I'm in a strange, deeply uncomfortable place in that one of my primary comforts through this process has been my spiritual work, but now the tools that were instrumental in getting to this point are causing no small amount of suffering as each time I use and consult them, they show me the bed I've made. I'm also faced with some resentment as others' that I am providing for are not doing this work, and it's hard to have the message be to "fix myself" when I'm so tired and still very much in the rat race. The hardest part is that I was generally an explorer/seeker, and go with the flow person, and now I'm wondering what it is I even want.

I've always thought of Saturn as a planet of debts to others, it's rather shocking to experience how broadly debt applies, and to see just how many commitments I thought of as minor or completely personal have spun into larger problems. I was experiencing the tip of this just after the new year, and made offering to Saturn pledging to better see and honor necessary limits. WHOA. I didn't know what I was in for.

As happens often nowadays, the act of formulating my question has helped me to answer it. I could use more OSA work, and perhaps to be more saturnine in my life, setting clear boundaries with work and family, and making small steps to honor the commitments to personal work. From you or the commentariat, any other ideas for:
A. Setting better boundaries with others.
B. Finding oneself again after years on autopilot in differing roles, father, son, husband, breadwinner, employee, etc.
C. Politely asking for a bit of reprieve from Saturn. (He does happen to be well aspected in my chart, though Mercury, Jupiter, and Uranus are not, I already thought of seeking some mitigating help from them.)

Third, and last (thank you for your patience with this longer post- it's been a hell of a month,) is that part of the past coming to visit is my father making an appearance after disowning me over a decade ago. Narcissism is a buzzword of late, but it is particularly helpful in describing him - he was solipsistic with an uncanny ability to conjure unique realities to fit his needs (and the harms he committed), leaving a messy wake through my family and my life. I've kept my distance with only texts and single voice call. He came in with the appearance of reconnecting, but old patterns quickly became apparent, and, thanks to my occult practice, I was able to withstand and navigate the lashing out, emotional triggers, and gaslighting. My calm defenses and clear communication obviously upset and unbalanced him and he has gone silent (leaving me with the last word which has never happened.) Since a couple days after, I've been exhausted, needing a nap everyday, and the rest of the troubles above have become far more overwhelming. One of the last times I saw him (over a decade ago) he mentioned magic practice and Thelema, which surprised me as he'd never shown any interest in study, spiritual practice, or the occult (other than loving Halloween and horror movies.) Even without magic, I believe in youth that I was one of his energy sources, and I'm sure there are pathways he could unconsciously exploit. I'm a bit concerned that I'm being targeted in some way, something I've never worried about before. I've noted that after my SOP, I feel tired rather than energized, but I am keeping up the SOP along with cold showers. I've just reviewed the FAQ and will work on a red bag, and I just finished The Witch of Criswell, so maybe some pepper in the shoes as well. I'm currently working primarily with the Golden Section, MOE, and AODA, though I worked through most of the first grade of the CGD. Any other ideas for protection or releasing myself from his energy?

On a final note - I loved The Witch of Criswell. I haven't done much pleasure reading in a good while, and it was a comfort during all the stress. I was prepared to be disappointed by it's brevity, when most twenty dollar books are 500 page tomes nowadays, but in the end, I appreciated being able to finish it in less than a month, and was grateful for the lack of fluff that fills so many of those novels; and even though it is a series, I was so grateful for an end to the story rather than an artificial break-point appended to a "cliffhanger." I very much appreciated that it is set in the real world, I didn't realize how tired I was of every story having some completely fantastical element, or secret powers, or being "chosen," rather than just a person having an experience that happens to be interesting and well told. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Going through the Ringer
replies.content:
First off, it sounds like some tough sledding lately, so please accept my sympathy and blessings if you'll have them.

Secondly, I've been wrestling with some similar, but not identical challenges, so here are a few thoughts to take or leave as seems best:

A) This can obviously be a challenge, especially with folks that you care about. First and foremost, it's helpful to find slack first where it's easiest - cut out time-consuming but low-value activities so that when you have to pick between two high-value things, you actually have time to do at least one of them. Basically, you'll need fewer hard boundaries if you have some wiggle room, and it's best to find the wiggle room where it hurts the least.

It can also help to talk over with the affected person/people what you need and why you think a boundary will help you get it. Obviously this is most true with those mature and close enough that you trust to have this discussion with. If your wife knows that you need an hour once a week for a ritual that she doesn't totally get, but the rest of the week you're your usual giving, helpful self, and having that time to yourself is part of what allows you to be so, it's a lot easier to accept.

Lastly, simple, easy-to-state and easy-to-understand rules or "policies" are an effective way to communicate to yourself and to others what your boundaries are and to make it easier to maintain them. If someone asks you for something, it's hard to say "no" to that particular request, but it's much easier (for most folks) to say "sorry, it's my policy not to take on those kinds of requests." It goes from being a personal judgment on the person asking you to being just part of your operating system that others will have to understand to deal with you. One from my work with students: I don't give feedback on documents over email. If you want me to review your resume, you have to make an in-person meeting, and we'll talk about it. Sure, some students are disappointed I can't "take a quick look" at something, but it beats telling all of my students "I don't offer feedback on stuff," or worse, leaving the emails to pile up unanswered with no explanation (though more of that happens than I'd prefer, so take all of this with the appropriate amount of salt).

B) I think most of us vastly underestimate how much our surroundings influence what we think we want and who we think we are. Roles offer the reassurance of specificity and clear expectations. I'm also struggling in this area (it's part of what's behind my thumos question above), so I don't have definitive answers unfortunately, but I do have three thoughts I can offer, in case they help:

B1) When it comes to deciding hard things, many of us don't actually want to decide, we want to go through some process that makes the right answer obvious, so that picking it becomes the only right choice, which you'll notice involves no actual deciding. But most decisions that really matter aren't that - you do have to pick, and at some level, you really don't know if it's the right one, and you might not ever know. Which sucks. But also, part of why you won't ever know is probably because there's rarely actually a "right" answer - whatever you pick, you'll learn and grow in some way, and chances are good you needed it at least a little.

B2) A thought that came to me in a recent meditation on the will, wanting, and so forth: "wanting" is like a fire, in that both change in the process. Deciding you "want" something, taking steps to get it, seeing what it's like along the way and once you get it all changes your relationship to the wanting itself, just as starting a fire, feeding it fuel, and seeing that fuel consumed changes the nature of the fire and your relationship to it. I haven't finished unpacking this one myself, but something suggested I should share it.

B3) If the people and context around us are setting much of what we want (or confusing us about the issue) then one obvious (but not easy) option is to find some time away from those people and context. If you can, find some time to get away from the things you feel have been on autopilot - take a guys' camping weekend, or use the summer to travel some, or put yourself on a news/blogging/whatever fast and read books by dead guys instead. To really, truly unplug likely takes at least a week or two, which may not be a luxury you can afford on some of these, but even mini-breaks can do a lot of good (when my family was stuck in our house during lockdowns, a weekly bath to myself became very important).

C) From everything I've read here and experienced myself, asking Saturn for "reprieve" may not be the best bet. Instead, I might emphasize to Him all of the things you are doing to live up to your pledge and getting in better touch with His energy, and point out your human frailty and limits (hey, look at that!). Also, you might meditate on/seek to connect with some of Saturn's less harsh aspects - provider of the harvest, overturner of social roles (Saturnalia), and yes, even rest after hard work.

At any rate, hope some of this helps, and good luck!
Jeff
